title: A Toolbox to Understand the Physics of Quantum Data Management
abstract: The application of quantum computing to data management has attracted growing interest, yet remains constrained by a limited understanding of how the physical behaviour of quantum devices relates to the structure and difficulty of database problems. In particular, evaluating quantum annealing approaches for combinatorial optimisation, which is central to many data management tasks, poses significant challenges beyond the scope of conventional empirical and complexity-theoretic methods.   We present a computational toolbox for the systematic numerical analysis of quantum annealing processes derived from data management problem formulations. Adopting a physics-informed perspective, the toolbox enables the study of spectral and dynamical properties -- such as energy gaps and eigenstate structure -- that are inaccessible through direct hardware measurements, yet essential for understanding computational hardness and scaling behaviour.   Our approach further provides derived quantities and visualisation techniques that support the interpretation of optimisation dynamics, the identification of structural similarities to canonical physical models, and the construction of reduced effective descriptions. By bridging methodological gaps between quantum computing and database systems research, this work establishes a principled foundation for evaluating quantum approaches and guiding future co-design efforts.
summary: This paper introduces an open-source computational toolbox designed to analyze the physical properties of quantum annealing applied to database management problems. By simulating energy gaps and spin dynamics, the authors show that query optimization problems possess a complexity structure intermediate between simple ferromagnets and complex spin glasses. This work establishes a foundation for the co-design of quantum algorithms and database systems.
main_problem: The lack of understanding regarding how the physical behavior of quantum annealing relates to the structure and difficulty of database management (DBMS) optimization problems.
main_result: The authors developed a computational toolbox that demonstrates MQO problems exhibit structured evolution intermediate between easy ferromagnetic models and hard spin-glass (SK) models, characterized by specific spectral gap behaviors and spin polarization patterns.
method: A physics-informed numerical analysis pipeline using High-Performance Computing (HPC) and matrix-free eigenvalue solvers (SLEPc/PETSc) to simulate the dynamics of time-dependent Hamiltonians.
model_or_system: A time-dependent Hamiltonian H(s) interpolating between a driver Hamiltonian and a problem-specific Ising/QUBO Hamiltonian, applied to Multi-Query Optimization (MQO) and Sherrington-Kirkpatrick (SK) spin-glass models.
key_observables: Minimum spectral gap, energy eigenvalues and eigenstates, spin expectation values, spin-spin correlations, and transition matrix elements.
important_parameters: Annealing parameter (s), system size (N), energy gaps, and the adiabatic condition ratio.
main_assumptions: The analysis uses a truncated low-energy subspace approximation, which may fail near avoided crossings or in dense spectra; the study focuses on standard linear annealing schedules.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the toolbox pipeline; Figures 2-3 compare spectral gaps and their locations for SK and MQO; Figures 4-5 show energy branch evolution; Figure 6 analyzes the adiabatic condition ratio; Figure 7 shows spin trajectories; Figure 9 displays spin-spin correlation patterns.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the disconnect between DBMS and quantum physics, presents the toolbox implementation and numerical methods, applies the tool to compare MQO against SK and Ferromagnetic models, and concludes with structural insights into optimization dynamics.
why_interesting: It provides a bridge between combinatorial optimization and the physics of phase transitions, specifically studying how the ruggedness of energy landscapes in disordered systems (like spin glasses) impacts the success of adiabatic protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of our Toolbox: Based on existing quantum formulations for optimisation problems that can be transcribed into a simple intermediate format, we use HPC systems to simulate the dynamics of a quantum annealing process. After post- processing the results (deliberately decoupled from the long-running and compute intensive simulation) to obtain quantities of interest, an accompanying set of visualisation scripts is used to understand the salient characteristics of the problem, and the implications on performance, achievable result quality, etc.; this can lead to (simplified) models, surrogates and others that allow for characterising properties of approaches, or predict...
